← the Atlas

Remote kaval endpoints — kolu dials (P3), the canvas (P4)

feature · seedling ·accepted ·JUN 15, 2026

The forward half of the kaval-sessions plan — kolu-server dialing a remote kaval over ssh (P3, decomposed into eight additive sub-PRs) and the multiplex canvas it unlocks (P4). Split out of kaval-sessions as the implementation-ready detail outgrew the direction note.

Contents

  • The remote process model (P3)
  • Implementing P3 — eight additive phases
    • The three remote seams
    • Where P3 stops and P4 begins
    • P3 pick-up anchors
    • Open calls — decide at implementation
  • P4 — the canvas: switch or multiplex

The forward half of kaval-sessions. That note carries the R-2 direction and the shipped spike (P0–P2.8, where kaval-tui --host already dials + provisions a remote kaval over ssh); this one is the implementation-ready plan for the two phases still ahead — P3 (kolu-server itself dials a remote kaval, so a canvas tile can live on another host) and P4 (the multiplex canvas on top). The substrate it builds on — one backend, three surfaces (PTY · fs · git) bound to an endpoint, reached by a unix-socket driver (local) or an ssh-stdio HostSession (remote) — is established in the parent; here it gets built.

The remote process model (P3)

kaval and kolu-watcher are two separate processes — forced, not incidental. kaval is the durable survivor (it must outlive deploys); kolu-watcher (provider DAG + fs/git) re-runs fresh (it must be the current build’s code — the #1031 line); one process cannot be both. Locally that role isn’t even a separate process — the DAG + fs/git watchers run inside kolu-server (local.ts: “the DAG runs HERE, in kolu-server”), with kaval the separate daemon. Remotely, kolu-server isn’t on the host, so it splits out as its own kolu-watcher process beside kaval.

And because kaval serves a unix socket (serveOverUnixSocket), reaching it over the network needs a remote stdio endpoint — and a HostSession is one ssh subprocess per host. So the remote host exposes one ssh endpoint — kolu-watcher: kolu-server dials it over one HostSession stdioLink, and kolu-watcher locally fronts kaval (its unix socket) for PTY attach/control + taps while serving its own fs/git + metadata surface (mirrorRemoteCollection). Concretely that is one composed @kolu/surface router served over serveOverStdio — kaval’s ptyHostSurface contract proxied through (kolu-watcher is a client of kaval’s socket) plus the watcher’s own fs/git/metadata — not the contract-blind frontDaemonOverStdio byte-relay kaval-tui uses (P2/P2.5): that relay eats the whole stdio to reach kaval alone, leaving no room for fs/git on the same link, so kolu-watcher cannot use it. The closure is shipped first via provisionAgent (nix copy --derivation → remote realise → pin a GC root). The diagram shows all three boundaries — host (dashed), process (boxed), package (packages/…):

If the host already has a kaval running, it is adopted — the same generic adoptOrEnsure spine (endpoint.ts), per endpoint, that the local boot uses. A duplicate is impossible by construction: a second launch hits the pid-gate’s atomic link(2) EEXIST, liveness-probes the holder (kill(pid,0)), and exits 0 (“yielding to the live instance”) without binding a socket — the running daemon is never disturbed. Re-provisioning is idempotent (nix realise no-ops if the closure is already present; an older build coexists and the moving GC root migrates to the new one). On dial:

the remote kaval is…kolu does
absentprovision + spawn fresh (adoptOrEnsure → ensure)
live · wire-compatibleadopt — connect, never kill; its PTYs reconcile in (older startedAt)
live · a build behindadopt + the B3.4 “update pending” nudge (an orthogonal axis)
live · contract-skewedrecycle (kill → respawn) — only on a typed DaemonContractSkewError, no retry
unreachable (bad dial)bounded retry, then degraded — never killed (a bad dial can’t cost live PTYs)

One kaval per remote host, adopted. Dialing a host joins its kaval — you reconnect to “the prod terminals” — and never clobbers it. The local per-port key (kaval-<port>/, #1313 ), which isolates two dev servers under always-recycle, isn’t needed across ssh: a remote host runs one shared kaval and dials adopt it (the table above), so two reconnects join the same terminals rather than racing to kill them. (The pid-gate is host-local — pid + filesystem on that host — not a cross-host or cross-container lock.)

Implementing P3 — eight additive phases

P3 is the largest phase: a re-introduced dispatch seam, additive wire/record fields, a brand-new host-resident process (kolu-watcher), the remote endpoint binding, three remote-only seams, and remote adoption. Ship it as eight sub-PRs, not one — the cadence the P2.x series already proved. Each phase is either a pure-additive change that keeps every local terminal byte-identical or a small user-facing increment, and the whole sequence is strictly additive — a local tile behaves identically at every step. The seam is already cut: TerminalEndpoint’s own doc-comment names “Remote (P3)” as “the same shape, a different transport,” and P0 ( #1364 ) left three call sites commented // P3 will select the endpoint…. P3.1 is the literal inverse of that commit.

  • P3.1–P3.2 are pure refactors — byte-identical, the suite stays green for the same reason P0 landed standalone.
  • P3.3–P3.4 add new, isolated code that changes no existing behavior (a contract type, a nix-config mirror, then the kolu-watcher process behind a throwaway spike) — additive, not yet user-reachable.
  • P3.5–P3.8 each deliver one small user-facing increment — a remote tile, a correct remote shell, the Code-tab + pills lighting up, surviving a restart.
PhaseKindShips — one PR eachGate
P3.1 · location resolverrefactorResurrect TerminalLocation = {kind:'local'} | {kind:'remote',host} in kolu-common/terminalEndpoint.ts; add a getTerminalEndpointFor(location) resolver in a new leaf server/src/terminalEndpoint/resolve.ts (a Map<hostId, endpoint> seeded local → localTerminalEndpoint, mirroring daemonStatus.ts). Route the three P0-commented call sites through it; remote arm throws. A create-time getTerminalEndpointForCreate({parentId, location}) reads the parent’s meta.location for sub-terminal inheritance.full suite green byte-for-byte; unit: local arm === the singleton, remote arm throws, sub-terminal inherits parent location; resolve.ts stays a leaf (preserves the #1005 import-order defense)
P3.2 · location on recordsrefactorAdd hostId to TerminalInfoSchema and location to the server-persisted metadata partition + TerminalCreateInputSchema — all .optional(), defaulting to local; rides SavedTerminal through the existing snapshot spread. Server stamps {kind:'local'} on every spawn; the client reads it but renders nothing.schema round-trip (a pre-P3 saved blob deserializes to local); the partition compile-fence; snapshot-then-delta unchanged; session-restore e2e green
P3.3 · watcher contract + drv mapadditiveA typed @kolu/surface kolu-watcher contract in kolu-common/watcher-surface.ts that composes two surfaces over one link: (a) kaval’s ptyHostSurface (spawn/attach/control + the cwd/title/foreground/exit taps), which kolu-watcher will proxy to the host’s kaval, and (b) the watcher-native surface — the TerminalEndpointFs/Git read+subscribe ops, the provider-DAG live outputs (agent/git/pr) as snapshot-then-delta, and a system.gateStatus() baked in for P3.8. + the per-system .drv map: a koluAgentDrvsJson sibling in flake.nix baked onto the server wrapper as KOLU_AGENT_DRVS_JSON (the exact mirror of KAVAL_AGENT_DRVS_JSON). Define the full contract now; later phases only fill handlers, never the wire. No process, no dial.just check typechecks the contract; a compile assertion that AgentClient<WatcherContract> exposes both the PTY and the fs/git/provider seams; nix build of the wrapper succeeds with openssh/nix on PATH; malformed drv-map throws synchronously at boot
P3.4 · kolu-watcher spikeadditiveThe kolu-watcher process (packages/kolu-watcher), PTY-only: it serves the P3.3 composed router over one serveOverStdio link, with only the PTY half live — those procedures proxied to the host’s durable kaval (kolu-watcher is a client of kaval’s unix socket; kaval is adopt-or-spawned via the @kolu/surface-daemon-supervisor spine), fs/git stubbed. This is deliberately not frontDaemonOverStdio (P2’s raw byte-relay can carry kaval and nothing else) — proving one link can demux proxied-kaval-PTY and watcher-native fs/git is the whole point. Driven by a throwaway ssh harness like the kaval-tui spike — touches no kolu-server call site. The keystone.falsifiability gate on a leased pu box: one link carries the proxied kaval PTY/taps and the watcher surface at once; spawn → attach → detach → re-dial reaches the same live PTY (kaval durability); every stream’s first yield is a full snapshot
P3.5 · first remote tileuser-facingRemoteTerminalEndpoint (server/src/terminalEndpoint/remote.ts) — the same TerminalEndpoint shape bound to the P3.4 watcher via getHostSession({ binary:'kolu-watcher', resolveDrvPath }); the resolver’s remote arm constructs/caches one per host. The first remote tile on the canvas (fs/git still stubbed). Proves the sync-shadow holds through a cold nix realise and that a bad host drives the session to failed and unwinds the shadow.evidence on live kolu + pu: a tile renders ~1 frame into copying, ready gates on the real remote PTY, keystrokes reach the remote shell; an un-provisionable host → failed, no orphaned entry, no hung spinner; local tiles byte-identical
P3.6 · remote-only seamsuser-facingInvert composeSpawnInput for remote (the remote host’s $SHELL/$HOME/$PATH win; only a presentation-var whitelist passes through — the proven buildRemoteCreateInput + REMOTE_ENV_PASSTHROUGH pattern) and guard paste/upload with PRECONDITION_FAILED on a remote tile.on pu: echo $SHELL $HOME shows the remote values, env shows no local /nix/store leakage; paste/upload on a remote tile → PRECONDITION_FAILED (with a “planned for P4” message), local unchanged
P3.7 · thicken kolu-watcheruser-facingMove kolu-watcher from PTY-only to full — this is the actual separation: the R-1.6 provider DAG + native fs/git now run inside kolu-watcher, on the remote host (agent/git/pr/foreground detection runs beside the JSONL files and repo it reads — the #1031 / proximity-of-data invariant), and mirror to kolu-server via mirrorRemoteCollection; replace P3.5’s stubs. The Code tab and the agent/git pills light up for remote tiles.on pu: a remote git-repo tile lists files, shows a real diff, the git pill resolves, and starting claude in the remote shell lights the agent pill — all derived on the remote host, not by reaching back over ssh per-op; a link-drop → respawn re-subscribes and re-paints the snapshot
P3.8 · remote adoptionuser-facingRemote adoption + boot reattach through the unchanged adoptOrEnsure/reconcile spine — a remote connect soul that runs the same contract-version handshake and reads the pid-gate over the wire (the RPC-baked system.gateStatus from P3.3); per-host DaemonStatus fed from session.onState. Remote terminals survive a kolu-server restart (adopt, never clobber).the five-state adoption table on pu (absent / wire-compatible / build-behind / contract-skewed / unreachable→degraded-never-killed); a server restart re-dials + reattaches surviving remote terminals with scrollback; daemonStatus shows the host’s transitions

Phasing note: P3.3 + P3.4 may merge into one “introduce kolu-watcher behind a spike” PR, and P3.4 may fold into P3.5 if you’d rather not land an unwired binary on master — the falsifiability gate is what matters, not the PR boundary. Everything else should stay split: each row is independently reviewable against a frozen seam and independently revertable.

The three remote seams

The “carries the remote seams” line, resolved concretely — each is small, and each is already de-risked by an existing pattern:

  • composeSpawnInput inversion (P3.6). Today the rich spawn policy (composeSpawnInput, wrapped by buildTerminalSpawnInput in server/src/ptyHost/index.ts) layers env with the local kolu-server process winning over the daemon’s system.info — correct on one machine, wrong over ssh (it would leak the local $HOME/$PATH//nix/store). The remote sibling inverts the precedence: the remote daemon’s system.info wins and only a presentation whitelist (TERM/LANG/LC_*/COLORTERM) rides through. This is exactly buildRemoteCreateInput + REMOTE_ENV_PASSTHROUGH from kaval-tui (P1/P2) — P3 lifts the pattern into kolu-server and routes by endpoint kind. The wire stays self-contained: no var refs, host-relative rc paths.
  • paste/upload PRECONDITION_FAILED guard (P3.6). pasteImage/uploadFile (router.ts) save into a local scratch dir and bracketed-paste that local path into the PTY — unreachable from a remote shell (a silent ENOENT the agent would chase). P3’s scope is the guard, not the fix: inspect the tile’s recorded location (on the record after P3.2) and throw PRECONDITION_FAILED on a remote tile. The actual copy-to-remote-scratch is P4.
  • drvPath via resolveSystem (P3.3 + P3.5). kolu-server must ship the right-arch kolu-watcher closure: resolveSystem(host) (the P2.8-memoized ssh arch probe) → a {system → .drv} map baked onto the server wrapper as KOLU_AGENT_DRVS_JSON (the exact mirror of kaval’s KAVAL_AGENT_DRVS_JSON), fed to getHostSession. The pool key is (host, binary) — one resolver per host.

Where P3 stops and P4 begins

P3 makes a remote terminal correct and observable on the server; P4 makes it discoverable, controllable, and fully featured in the UI. So P3 ends with hostId on the wire that the client reads but renders nothing host-aware — tiles, dock and chrome are visually byte-identical for local and remote. P4 adds the active-kaval strip, the per-tile host hint, the ssh-config picker, the reconnect affordance (a button wired to session.reconnect()), and the real paste/upload-to-remote that turns P3.6’s guard into a feature. The sharp line: anything that renders a host indicator, a picker, or a recovery control — or transfers a file to the remote host — is P4.

P3 pick-up anchors

Concrete hooks, every path verified against the tree (the P1/P2 anchor tables in the parent set the convention):

ConcernWhere it lands
dispatch seamTerminalLocation back in packages/common/src/terminalEndpoint.ts (its doc already names the remote binding); new leaf packages/server/src/terminalEndpoint/resolve.ts (the inverse of the deleted terminalBackend/index.ts, commit 1c8f1770); re-route terminals.ts (the // P3 will select… comment), surface.ts (the fs/git stream block), router.ts (terminal.attach)
wire / recordpackages/common/src/surface.ts — hostId on TerminalInfoSchema, location on ServerPersistedTerminalFieldsSchema (.optional()); TerminalCreateInputSchema in contract.ts. Heed the metadata-partition comment — misclassifying a new field onto the wrong base is the one silent failure mode
remote transportimport getHostSession · HostSession · resolveSystem · mirrorRemoteCollection · makeClientCursor · isLocalHost · FailureCause from packages/surface-nix-host/src/index.ts; new packages/server/src/terminalEndpoint/remote.ts; new packages/common/src/watcher-surface.ts; new packages/kolu-watcher — the host-resident process. It serves a composed router via serveOverStdio (@kolu/surface): the PTY half a client of kaval’s unix socket (kaval adopt-or-spawned via @kolu/surface-daemon-supervisor’s adoptOrEnsure), the fs/git/metadata half native (kolu-git on the remote disk + the unchanged DAG from terminalEndpoint/providers.ts). Not frontDaemonOverStdio — that’s P2’s kaval-only raw relay
the three seamscomposeSpawnInput/buildTerminalSpawnInput in packages/server/src/ptyHost/index.ts; inversion pattern at packages/kaval-tui/src/create.ts (buildRemoteCreateInput + REMOTE_ENV_PASSTHROUGH); paste guard at router.ts (pasteImage/uploadFile/bracketedPastePath); resolveSystem in surface-nix-host/src/arch.ts
adoption spine (reused unchanged)adoptOrEnsure in packages/surface-daemon-supervisor/src/endpoint.ts; pure partition in packages/server/src/reconcile.ts; adoptSurvivingSession in terminalEndpoint/reattach.ts (parallel the remote one); the local connect soul to mirror in ptyHost/connect.ts; DaemonStatus already keyed by hostId in ptyHost/daemonStatus.ts
nixflake.nix (kavalAgentDrvsJson → add a koluAgentDrvsJson sibling) + default.nix (the KAVAL_AGENT_DRVS_JSON --set-default line → add KOLU_AGENT_DRVS_JSON, plus openssh/nix on the server wrapper PATH)

Open calls — decide at implementation

Genuine design calls left to the implementer, each with a recommendation:

  1. Where fs/git lives — kolu-watcher runs kolu-git natively + the DAG in-process; kaval stays a pure PTY host (co-locates agent detection with the JSONL files it reads). Recommended.
  2. Remote gate read — an RPC-baked system.gateStatus() on the watcher contract, not an ssh-exec subprocess; bake it into the P3.3 contract so P3.8 needs no wire bump. Recommended.
  3. Pooling — one HostSession per remote host, keyed (host, 'kolu-watcher'), shared across the PTY + fs/git + metadata pumps. Recommended.
  4. Create-time selection — explicit location > parent inheritance > default-local; the interactive picker is P4. Recommended.
  5. Remote list failure — degrade, don’t recycle (a pure-dial remote may not be kolu’s to own — unlike the local F3 “recycle on list failure”, which assumes kolu owns the lifecycle). Recommended; settle in P3.8.

P4 — the canvas: switch or multiplex

With P3 the server can hold N live endpoints; P4 spends that on the UI — and settles the one open granularity call. The same substrate answers either, so it is a later, reversible choice that the P1–P2 spike deliberately did not depend on:

switch (tmux-style)multiplex (canvas) — leaning
the canvasone kaval in viewtiles span kavals
per-tile locationunneededadditive — the R-1 discriminator, finally used
live endpointsone, swappedN concurrent
host hintone current pointera light per-tile tag; ChromeBar shows the active kavals
why it fits kolusimplestthe canvas is spatial + heterogeneous by design

The lean is multiplex: tmux-switch is a terminal-multiplexer metaphor, but kolu is a canvas — “watch a local build beside a prod-ssh tile” is the point of a canvas, not a corner case. The cost over switch is modest and clean — per-tile location returns, but additively (no RemoteTerminalBackend, no screen mirror — just which endpoint holds this tile’s PTY), and the ChromeBar shows only the kavals this canvas actually has tiles on.

P4 builds multiplex, then validates the feel. The UI work consumes exactly the server substrate P3 left: the active-kaval strip (local always present), the per-tile host hint, the ssh-config picker (how a user chooses a remote host — P3’s selection is config/env-driven, not interactive), the reconnect affordance wired to session.reconnect() and tooltip’d from lastError, and turning P3.6’s paste/upload PRECONDITION_FAILED guard into a real feature (copy the file to the remote scratch dir, bracketed-paste the remote path). Then the validation: does N-at-once actually beat tmux-switch in daily use? Fall back to switch if multiplex doesn’t earn its keep — the granularity call lands and is validated here, on real use, not up front.

← the Atlas github ↗
